title: Config loader: precedence chain + per-field deep merge
description: Resolve effective config across layers. Load ~/.tldr/config.json (global, optional) then <project>/.tldr/config.json (project), deep-merge per field (project wins), then apply CLI-flag overrides (flag wins) as the final layer. Missing file at any layer = no-op. Project root resolution should match existing tldr conventions (the dir containing .tldr / .git / manifest). Output: a single resolved config struct the commands consume. Tests: global-only, project-overrides-global, CLI-overrides-both, all-absent = built-in defaults.
close_reason: Added merge() method for deep per-field merge (project wins over global), resolve() method that loads ~/.tldr/config.json then <project>/.tldr/config.json with deep merge. 5 tests: project-overrides-global, preserves-unset-fields, semantic-disabled-wins, all-absent-defaults, no-project-root.

title: Define TldrConfig serde structs + tolerant JSON parsing
close_reason: Added TldrConfig, EmbeddingConfig, SemanticConfig serde structs in tldr-core/src/config.rs with serde(default) on all fields, no deny_unknown_fields. from_str + from_path (returns default on missing file). 7 unit tests: empty {}, partial, unknown-keys-ignored, full cloud-shaped, missing file, malformed JSON, semantic disabled.

title: Per-project config file (.tldr/config.json) for embedding model + settings
description:
Goal: let users change the embedding model (and future settings) per project WITHOUT recompiling. Today the model is hardcoded (default_value="arctic-m" in semantic.rs:36 / embed.rs; impl Default for EmbeddingModel). No config-file machinery exists in tldr-code (no toml dep, no loader, no app Config struct).

Design (settled):
- ONE JSON file per project: .tldr/config.json. Optional global ~/.tldr/config.json.
- Precedence (deep per-field merge): CLI flag > .tldr/config.json > ~/.tldr/config.json > built-in default. Missing file/field = built-in default = today's behavior.
- Tolerant parsing: serde(default) on everything, NO deny_unknown_fields — ignore unknown keys so newer configs don't break older binaries (forward-compat).
- Schema (single embedding block, provider-discriminated so cloud slots in with no restructure):
  { version:int=1,
    embedding: { provider:"local", model:"arctic-l", endpoint?:str, api_key_env?:str, dimensions?:int },
    semantic:  { enabled:bool=true, langs?:[str] } }
- Built-in default model stays arctic-m (conservative; small/fast). Config opts up to arctic-l etc.
- Note: the semantic EMBEDDING cache is GLOBAL (~/.tldr/embeddings/cache.json) keyed by abs file_path:function:hash:model — per-project model choices coexist safely. Structural Salsa cache is per-project (.tldr/cache).
- No JSON-Schema validation file; loader validates at runtime.

This feature umbrella tracks the sub-tasks.
close_reason: All sub-tasks complete: TldrConfig structs (qw2), config loader with precedence chain (hzv), and embedder wiring with provider seam (5xn). Per-project .tldr/config.json is now supported with global ~/.tldr/config.json fallback and deep merge. CLI flag > project config > global config > built-in default. Cloud provider fails fast with clear error.

title: Install tldr diagnostic tools for supported languages
description:
tldr-code has optional diagnostic tools (doctor, diagnostics) that require external language tools to be installed. Need to run 'tldr doctor --install <language>' for each supported language in the project.

**Problem**: The current install flow does NOT mention these optional tools anywhere. Users (and agents) have no visibility into these additional capabilities, so they never get installed or used. This means diagnostic features like 'tldr doctor' and language-specific analysis are silently unavailable.

**What needs to happen**:
1. Update the install flow/documentation to include an 'Optional Diagnostic Tools' step
2. Make it clear which tools are available per language and how to install them
3. Ensure agents and users discover these capabilities during setup

Languages and their tools:
- Python: pyright, ruff, mypy
- TypeScript: typescript-language-server, tsc
- Go: gopls, golangci-lint
- Rust: rustc, cargo
- Java: checkstyle, spotbugs

Install command: tldr doctor --install <language>

Steps:
1. Run 'tldr doctor' to check current state
2. Add optional tools section to the install flow
3. Install tools for languages used in this project (Rust is primary)
4. Verify installation with 'tldr doctor' again
notes: Discovered during session — the entire workflow (CLAUDE.md, setup docs, onboarding) is missing any mention of these optional diagnostic tools. This is a gap in discoverability, not just installation.
close_reason: Updated CLAUDE.md with build commands, diagnostic tools section (tldr doctor --install), architecture overview, and conventions. INSTALL.md already had the optional tools section; SETUP.md already mentions tldr doctor. The gap was agent instructions not surfacing these capabilities.

title: Python/TS/generic resolvers: same per-call-site-scan defect class as Gate-1 rounds 1-5 (unmeasured; ADR-6 decision input)
description:
The Gate-1 campaign (compose 223s -> 0.56s on rust) fixed SEVEN defects of one class: per-call-site linear work that should be indexed lookups. The rust fixes were: find_by_name secondary index, RustReceiverIndex (per-file binding/impl index, then fully inverted incl. weird receivers), find_by_name routing in resolve_local_fuzzy_match / resolve_type_aware_fallback.

The NON-rust paths still run the legacy shape: resolve_receiver_type (resolution.rs) dispatches per call site to per-language scanners (python/TS/JS/Go/Java/... in type_resolver.rs) that re-scan source text per call — the exact pattern rounds 2-4 killed for rust. Generic paths fixed in round 5 (local_fuzzy/type_aware_fallback) already benefit all languages.

SCOPE: (1) MEASURE first (ADR-3 rule — no fixing before profiling): TLDR_PHASE_TIMING=1 build on a large python and a large TS corpus; (2) if compose dominates, apply the inverted-index treatment per language with the same layered differential gates (ADR-5: exhaustive synthetic + corpus-level value identity + count identity + determinism).

WHY IT MATTERS: one of exactly two missing inputs to the Path A/B call in ADR-6 (TLDR-7mp) — per-language compose economics may differ materially from rust's.

title: ADR-7: Memory attribution correction — headline peak is EMBED phase; chunk store does not address it
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-05) Stop justifying the chunk store with the headline memory number. The measured 27.0GB peak belongs to the EMBED phase (Flow-2: ONNX inference + accumulate-then-save). Memory work routes accordingly: TLDR-3rh/TLDR-yll own the embed peak; the F-series surgical fixes (F1/TLDR-9ae shipped; F2-F4/TLDR-1in pending) own the callgraph-phase transient.

## Rationale (correction owned)
We carried "the 22GB is the callgraph cache; the chunk store kills it" forward in the benefits statement. Our own measurements say otherwise:
- The callgraph-phase plateau was 11.4GB PRE-F1 (with the to_value DOM); F1 is shipped and the post-F1 number is unmeasured.
- The true daemon peak = max(phase peaks) = the EMBED phase, untouched by any chunk-store design.
- 27.0GB vs the historical 22.7GB is partly BETTER MEASUREMENT, not worse behavior: 27.0 came from the getrusage high-water probe (true max over 4.5h), 22.7 from 15s ps polling (under-samples peaks by construction). Provenance: the overnight diff touched only callgraph/* — semantic/ ran byte-identical to main.

## Alternatives Considered
- Keep quoting the headline number for the chunk store: rejected — it assigns another subsystem's peak to this design and would make Path B look better than it is.

## Status: ACTIVE.

title: ADR-6: Gate-1 PASS — recompose-always stands; chunk-store time case reduced to ~2.7s; Path A/B deferred to two measurements
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-05, supersedes ADR-1's justification) Gate-1 PASSES: compose = 0.56s, under the 2s recompose-per-edit line with margin. Recompose-always v1 stands AS DESIGNED — no escalation needed. HOWEVER the chunk store's TIME justification is re-derived honestly: a full rebuild is now ~3.1s (parse 2.7s + compose 0.56s), so chunk persistence buys ONLY the ~2.7s parse memoization. The Path A (surgical fixes, no storage layer) vs Path B (full two-flow chunk store) decision is DEFERRED until two missing measurements exist:
1. Post-F1 warm memory peak (callgraph-phase transient after the to_value DOM removal) — ~22s measurement, needs daemon authorization.
2. Python/TS resolver compose numbers — same per-call-site-scan defect class, unmeasured; per-language economics may differ materially.

## Rationale
The problem ADR-1 designed for (220s+ rebuilds, minutes-long restarts) largely DISSOLVED under measurement and defect fixes. What Path B uniquely retains regardless: cross-process/CI chunk sharing (~parse only), mmap-evictable steady state, restart without parse. Whether that justifies weeks of storage work is a memory question now, not a time question.

## Alternatives Considered
- Proceed with Path B on the original benefits table: rejected — the table conflated defect costs with design value ("restart 3.5min -> seconds" was mostly the defects).
- Kill Path B now: rejected — memory + multi-language numbers could still justify it; deferring costs nothing.

## Status: ACTIVE — the current architectural position.

title: ADR-5: Output-identity discipline — perf fixes proven by layered differential gates, never by 'tests pass'
description:
## Decision
(overnight 2026-06-05, formalized in round 5) Every performance fix to resolution logic must be OUTPUT-IDENTICAL and PROVEN so, by layered gates: (1) exhaustive synthetic differential vs the legacy implementation, (2) frozen-corpus count identity (745/20,320/26,312 across all rounds), (3) corpus-level VALUE identity on real call-site receivers (round-5 closer, advisor-prompted: counts cannot catch same-count-different-target divergence), (4) run-to-run determinism of the full 8.4MB edge output.

## Rationale
A 400x speedup that silently changes which edges resolve is a correctness regression wearing a perf medal. The legacy implementation stays in-tree as the reference oracle.

## Alternatives Considered
- "Counts match + suite green" alone: rejected after advisor review — it leaves exactly one divergence mode open (same count, different resolved type).

## Bonus outcome
The discipline also REMOVED pre-existing nondeterminism: the old fuzzy-match paths picked among candidates in HashMap iteration order (random per process); the indexed rewrite is deterministic (TLDR-2af direction).

## Status: ACTIVE.

title: ADR-4: Treat Gate-1 FAIL (223s) as suspected algorithmic defect — profile/fix loop before architecture escalation
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-04 night) On Gate-1 failing at compose=223s (110x over the 2s line), do NOT escalate to heavier architecture (query-time resolution / incremental compose / Glean-style ownership sets). Instead run profile -> name hot path -> output-identical fix -> differential gate -> re-bench loops on the EXISTING pipeline.

## Rationale
223s / 26,312 edges = ~8.5ms per edge — pathological vs comparable tools (ty type-checks all of home-assistant in 5.3s; rust-analyzer resolves workspaces in seconds). That smelled like implementation defects, not inherent cost. Profiling is cheap; escalation stays available if fixes stall.

## Alternatives Considered
- Escalate immediately per the pre-planned ladder: rejected — would have built complex machinery on top of unmeasured defects, repeating ADR-1's mistake at a higher level.

## Outcome (VINDICATED — 400x)
5 rounds, 7 defects, ALL the same class (per-call-site linear scans that should be indexed lookups; all present since initial release): compose 223s -> 0.56s; build 226s -> 3.1s. Gate-1 PASSED with the original architecture intact — the escalation ladder was never needed.

## Method lessons baked in (mistakes owned)
- Round 1 fixed a REAL defect (find_by_name linear scan) and got ZERO speedup — it was not the dominant branch. Lesson: sum ALL profile branches before fixing; do not fix the first named function.
- Round 2's own fix (the receiver index) became round 5's hot path. Lesson: re-profile your own fixes; an index that replaces a scan can hide a smaller scan inside itself.

## Status: ACTIVE — standing method for perf campaigns on this codebase.

title: ADR-3: No status above PROPOSED until viability gates are MEASURED; no storage code before Gate-0/1
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-04 evening, supersedes ADR-2's lock) Process rule: architecture decisions stay PROPOSED-PENDING-GATE until their kill-gates are measured on the real pipeline. Concretely: no storage code before Gate-0 (FileIR purity) and Gate-1 (compose cost <=2s). Compose model corrected to LAZY (on-query-after-dirty) in the same review.

## Rationale
A gate that can kill the design must run before anything is "locked". Adversarial self-review called the prior locking "manufactured false certainty"; the advisor independently re-classified Gate-1 from open-question to approval-blocker.

## Alternatives Considered
- Keep LOCKED, treat Gate-1 as a formality: rejected — that is how weeks get spent building a cache for the cheap step.

## Outcome (VINDICATED — this rule did the work)
Gate-1 ran the same evening and FAILED 110x over the line: the unmeasured premise (parse expensive / compose cheap) was exactly backwards. Zero storage code had been written. The entire cost of the wrong assumption was one evening of measurement instead of weeks of building.

## Status: ACTIVE — this is now the standing rule for TLDR-zde and successor designs.

title: ADR-2: Mark architecture LOCKED before running Gate-1 (process mistake, owned)
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-04 afternoon) Mark the chunk-store architecture choices — two-flow constraint, rkyv chunks, CSR+fst layout, lifecycle, integrity scheme — as LOCKED while Gate-1 (the compose-cost bench) was still UNRUN.

## Rationale (at the time)
Choices were user-confirmed and research-double-confirmed (incl. a counter-evidence pass); locking was meant to stop re-litigating settled questions.

## Why this was a mistake (owned)
Gate-1 decided whether the recompose-based design was viable AT ALL — it was an approval blocker mislabeled as an open question. Locking around an unrun measurement manufactured false certainty. This is the central PROCESS error of the campaign: certainty was assigned by research consensus, not by measurement.

## How it was caught
Adversarial self-review + advisor, the same day, BEFORE the bench ran: "downgrade all LOCKED items to PROPOSED-PENDING-GATE-1". Hours later Gate-1 failed 110x over the pass line, proving the downgrade right.

## Lesson
Research can validate that a library is healthy; only measurement can validate that a design premise is true. Lock nothing upstream of an unrun kill-gate.
close_reason: Superseded by TLDR-k89 (ADR-3): LOCKED downgraded to PROPOSED-PENDING-GATE-1 before the gate ran

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: SUPERSEDED by TLDR-k89 (ADR-3) the same day, before Gate-1 ran: adversarial self-review downgraded all LOCKED items to PROPOSED-PENDING-GATE-1. Hours later Gate-1 failed 110x over the line, proving the downgrade right. Kept on record deliberately: locking around an unrun kill-gate is the process mistake this chain exists to remember.

title: ADR-1: Chunk-store design premised on 'parse expensive, compose cheap' (unmeasured)
description:
## Decision
(2026-06-04, original TLDR-zde design) Build a disk-backed, per-file chunk-invalidated analysis cache: persist each file's parse output (FileIR) keyed by content hash; on edit, re-parse ONE chunk and re-run global compose; restart loads chunks via mmap in seconds.

## Rationale (as believed at the time — the core ASSUMPTION, never measured)
- Parse/extract was assumed to be the expensive, memoizable step; global compose assumed to be a small fraction of build cost ("expected: small fraction of parse cost, but verify" — the 'verify' is what saved us).
- Whole-project in-RAM JSON blobs caused 22GB build peaks, full recompute on any edit, 3.5min daemon restarts; per-file chunks fix the granularity mismatch.

## Alternatives Considered
- Redis-class KV, Arrow hot path, graph DBs, heap petgraph, adjacency matrices, JSON-as-storage, salsa crate v1, stack-graphs crate — all rejected in the research round (see TLDR-zde design notes + research annex).

## What happened (transparency record)
- Gate-1 (2026-06-04 evening) measured the premise INVERTED: parse 2.8s, compose 223s = 98.8% of build. Memoizing parse saves ~1% of build time.
- The 223s was then proven to be SEVEN implementation defects, not inherent cost (rounds 1-5 overnight+morning: compose 223s -> 0.56s, output-identical, all defects dated to initial release).
- The architecture itself survived (Gate-1 ultimately PASSED at 0.56s) but this decision's TIME justification collapsed to ~2.7s parse memoization on a 3.1s rebuild.

## Lesson
We designed a cache for the step we assumed was expensive. Measurement showed we had it exactly backwards — and that the expensive step was only expensive because it was buggy.
close_reason: Superseded by TLDR-7mp (ADR-6): core premise measured inverted by Gate-1; time case re-derived honestly

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: SUPERSEDED by TLDR-7mp (ADR-6): the premise 'parse expensive, compose cheap' was measured exactly inverted by Gate-1 (parse 2.8s / compose 223s), and after the defect campaign (compose -> 0.56s) the time justification reduced to ~2.7s parse memoization. Kept on record deliberately: this is the assumption the whole design rested on, stated as we believed it.

title: bound Flow-2 cold-embed memory: ONNX arena/batch tuning (measured ~11.5GB resident during cold inference)
description: After the Flow-1 redesign (TLDR-zde, build peak target ≤3GB), the daemon's worst memory moment becomes the semantic cold-embed phase: measured ~11.5GB resident during ONNX inference (vs 3.4GB cache-hit path) on 28,200 vectors. Knobs to evaluate: embedding batch size, ONNX Runtime arena config (arena shrink/extend strategy), intra-op thread count, possibly streaming persist of embedded chunks instead of accumulate-then-save. Acceptance: cold embed peak ≤4GB without materially extending the ~90min cold build time. Sequential phases ⇒ daemon peak = max(phase peaks), so this caps the whole daemon.

title: Onboarding: one-command install + auto MCP/hook registration per agent
description:
From GrapeRoot competitive analysis (TLDR-2o4), finding #5.

PROBLEM: GrapeRoot is one curl install then 'dgc .' — auto-writes .cursor/mcp.json, .vscode/mcp.json, opencode.json, Claude settings hooks for six agents. tldr needs a Rust toolchain or release binary, manual MCP registration per agent, tldr doctor for language tools. Time-to-first-value gap is large for non-expert users.

PROPOSAL (incremental, no auto-update-from-CDN — that's their supply-chain liability, not a feature to copy):
- install script for release binaries (checksummed, pinned versions)
- 'tldr setup --claude|--cursor|--codex|...' writes the right MCP config/hook entries idempotently
- 'tldr setup' with no args detects which agents are installed and offers each
- fold doctor checks into the setup flow output

title: Compaction recovery: re-inject codebase context after Claude Code compacts
description:
From GrapeRoot competitive analysis (TLDR-2o4), finding #3.

PROBLEM: when Claude Code compacts mid-conversation, codebase context evaporates and the agent rediscovers everything — with or without tldr installed. GrapeRoot installs hooks that re-inject context on compaction. (Same failure mode bd prime's SessionStart hook exists for, but for code context.)

PROPOSAL: a SessionStart hook (source=compact) / PreCompact hook that asks the daemon for a compact project re-orientation pack: project shape, the session-memory hot set (sibling issue), key signatures. Cheap to build once pre-injection + session memory exist — this is mostly a second trigger for the same packer.

title: Session memory: track per-conversation reads/edits so follow-ups skip retrieval
description:
From GrapeRoot competitive analysis (TLDR-2o4), finding #2.

PROBLEM: our daemon caches ANALYSIS (Salsa, warmed caches) but knows nothing about the SESSION. Every question is a cold query even if the agent asked about the same file 30s ago. GrapeRoot keeps a live action graph of what was read/edited/decided during the conversation and routes follow-ups to previously-touched files with confidence=high, no retrieval.

PROPOSAL: lightweight per-session state in the daemon — session ID keyed, tracking files/symbols served, edits observed (we already watch the FS), and recency/frequency. Pre-injection (sibling issue) consults it first: follow-up turns get previously-touched context instantly and spend the remaining budget on NEW material only. Depends on pre-injection landing first to have a consumer.

title: Competitive analysis: GrapeRoot — learn from their delivery/lifecycle model
description:
We did a competitive product analysis of GrapeRoot (graperoot.dev, 'Dual-Graph') on 2026-06-04 and decided to learn from them and improve ourselves. These are the findings.

WHO THEY ARE: local-first codebase-context tool for Claude Code / Codex / Cursor / Gemini / Copilot / OpenCode. Builds a code graph (files/functions/classes, import+call edges, keyword scoring), packs ~4k-token structured summaries (signatures, call edges, inline top-function bodies), and PRE-INJECTS them into the agent prompt in ~150ms via hooks — zero tool calls on the happy path.

KEY STRATEGIC DATA POINT: they STARTED as an MCP server (graph_continue/graph_read tools) and pivoted AWAY because per-call MCP protocol overhead (tool defs, routing, formatting) compounded across turns and ate the savings. That is empirical, experience-based evidence against the pull-only position tldr-mcp occupies in the loop.

WHERE THEY ARE BETTER (gaps to close, each a child issue):
1. Push vs pull delivery — pre-injection via hooks vs our tool-call-only model
2. Session memory — they track reads/edits/decisions during the conversation; our daemon caches analysis but knows nothing about the session
3. Compaction recovery — hooks re-inject context when Claude Code compacts; we lose everything
4. Cross-session persistence — decisions/WHY memory injected at session start; no cold start
5. Onboarding friction — one curl install, auto-config for six agents; we need cargo/binary + manual MCP registration + tldr doctor
6. Live session cost visibility — passive dashboard, 'what's my cost so far?' mid-session; our telemetry only measures tldr's own savings

WHERE WE ARE BETTER (do not regress): analysis depth (CFG/DFG/taint/call-graph/semantic search incl. measured modality-gap fixes) vs their keyword scoring; no auto-update-from-CDN supply-chain surface; published/verifiable behavior (their benchmarks page is empty).

TAKEAWAY: they win on everything AROUND the analysis (delivery, session lifecycle, onboarding); we win on the analysis itself. Items 1-3 are architecture gaps, not feature gaps. Our daemon already has warm caches and a fast answer path — a hook-based pre-injection mode on top of it neutralizes most of their advantage.

Raw research: .firecrawl/graperoot-docs.md, .firecrawl/graperoot-setup.md (session-local). Source: graperoot.dev/docs, graperoot.dev/setup, GitHub kunal12203/Codex-CLI-Compact.

title: status Memory probe: report phys_footprint (not just resident) + migrate rss.rs to mach2
description: Observed 2026-06-04: status said 'Memory: 13.7 GB (peak 16.0)' while actual phys_footprint was 22.0GB — mach_task_basic_info resident_size underreports when macOS compresses idle pages. Fix: read phys_footprint (task_vm_info.phys_footprint) and report it alongside/instead of RSS; TLDR-yll part-3 max-RSS policy must gate on footprint or it will under-trigger exactly when pressure is highest. Same file fix: rss.rs:19,26 uses deprecated libc::mach_task_self_ (2 build warnings) — migrate to the mach2 crate while in there.

title: disk-backed per-file chunk-invalidated salsa cache (replace whole-project in-RAM entries)
description: Full design sketch in TLDR-k8s design notes §5. Today: in-RAM DashMap of WHOLE-PROJECT JSON blobs; one changed file nukes the entire entry (full 22GB recompute); daemon restart loses everything. Proposal: persist per-FILE chunks (FileIR) keyed by (path, content_hash) in compact binary (rkyv → mmap zero-copy); project queries become compose-over-chunks (only cross-file resolution recomputes); file change → recompute ONE chunk (mirrors t8f semantic delta — invalidate_by_input plumbing already exists, only storage granularity + compose step are new); restart → seconds of mmap instead of 3.5min + 22GB. Open questions: measure standalone compose cost; schema versioning; atomic write via vector_store generation pattern; chunk enumeration must share corpus rules with TLDR-1qv/TLDR-kkt or caches drift.
design:
# DECISION STATE (consolidated 2026-06-05, post Gate-1 PASS — supersedes the 2026-06-04 consolidation; full evolution in the ADR chain TLDR-cv9..ab6, index comment 2026-06-05)

## MEASURED FACTS (rust on tldr-code, 745 files / 20,320 funcs / 26,312 edges)
- Phase split: scan ~10ms / parse 2.5-2.8s / compose 0.56s / total ~3.1s.
- Original premise "parse expensive, compose cheap" was INVERTED (ADR-1, closed):
  baseline compose was 223s = 98.8% of build — but that was SEVEN defects of one
  class (per-call-site linear scans -> indexed lookups), all dated to initial
  release, fixed output-identical across 5 rounds (commits 100f465/8ca8f0c/
  106738d/<round-5>). Campaign net: compose 400x, build 73x.
- GATE-1: PASS (0.56s < 2s line). Gate-0 (FileIR purity): PASS (earlier).
- Correctness: exhaustive synthetic differential + corpus-level value-identity
  gate (real call-site receivers) + frozen-corpus count identity + 8.4MB edge
  JSON deterministic run-to-run (old code was HashMap-order random).

## CURRENT ARCHITECTURAL POSITION (ADR-6 / TLDR-7mp)
- Recompose-always v1 STANDS as designed; escalation ladder (query-time
  resolution / incremental compose / Glean sets) NOT needed.
- BUT the chunk store's TIME case is now only ~2.7s parse memoization on a
  3.1s rebuild. Time alone no longer justifies Path B.
- MEMORY attribution corrected (ADR-7 / TLDR-ab6): headline 27GB peak is the
  EMBED phase (TLDR-3rh/yll), untouched by any chunk-store design; the
  callgraph transient was 11.4GB PRE-F1, post-F1 unmeasured.

## PATH FORK — DEFERRED pending exactly two measurements (ADR-6)
1. TLDR-myd: post-F1 warm memory peak (~22s run; needs resident-daemon auth).
2. TLDR-olg: python/TS resolver phase split (same defect class, unmeasured).
Decision rule: if F1 already took most of the callgraph transient AND other
languages respond to the same index treatment, Path A (surgical, no storage
layer) wins; Path B retains cross-process/CI chunk sharing (~parse only),
mmap-evictable steady state, restart-without-parse (~2.7s).

## STANDING RULES (active ADRs)
- ADR-3 (TLDR-k89): nothing above PROPOSED until kill-gates are MEASURED;
  no storage code before gates. (Replaced the LOCKED status of 2026-06-04.)
- ADR-4 (TLDR-w9z): pathological perf numbers = suspected defect first;
  profile->fix->re-bench before architecture escalation. Sum ALL profile
  branches; re-profile your own fixes.
- ADR-5 (TLDR-f0n): perf fixes proven output-identical via layered
  differential gates; legacy implementation retained as oracle.

## TECH CHOICES (status: PROPOSED, unchanged content)
rkyv 0.8.x chunks / fst symbol index / CSR fwd+rev / mmap zero-copy /
generation-commit lifecycle / ignore-first membership (TLDR-bpf oracle) /
bytecheck-once + header checksum. Rejections stand (Redis-class KV, Arrow hot
path, graph DBs, heap petgraph, JSON-as-storage, salsa v1, stack-graphs crate).
A6 format spike (rkyv vs epserde for CSR artifact) only relevant if Path B.

## POST-V1 PARKED (unchanged)
TLDR-38j oxc_resolver spike; Parquet/DataFusion analytics sidecar; Codex
independent review re-run (brief: /tmp/tldr-analysis-store-review-brief.md).

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: ADVISOR REVIEW (interim; Codex retry pending — backend 503): architecture endorsed (parse-memoized-per-file + always-global-compose = standard incremental-compiler shape, mirrors vector store). TWO VIABILITY GATES: Gate 0 — FileIR must be pure parse output: VERIFIED 2026-06-04, resolution phase takes &file_ir immutably (builder_v2.rs:924,953) and writes resolved edges to separate structures (956); compose iterates ir.files.iter() as shared refs — no write-back, chunks cannot cache stale resolution. Gate 1 — compose-only cost bench (steps 5-12 over pre-built FileIRs) is an APPROVAL BLOCKER, not an open question: full-recompose-per-delta is only viable if global compose is ~sub-2s; if compose is a large fraction of build time, incremental compose is forced into v1 (much bigger design). NOTE this is NOT what TLDR-335 measures (that's parse-plateau allocation attribution) — separate cheap spike needed. PRECISION: this design kills the TRANSIENT 22GB build peak (ASTs+DOM); steady-state resident becomes composed-graph size in ArcSwap — bounded but uncharacterized; idle/warm was already fine at 1.4GB. NAMING: drop 'salsa v2' label — Q10 (adopt real salsa crate for compose layer?) shouldn't be prejudged by the name.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text:
# Research: 2026 state-of-the-art for persistent, zero-copy, incremental analysis caches
## TLDR-zde research annex — sources scraped 2026-06-04 via Firecrawl (.firecrawl/)
## Status: research base. Codex review (pending retry) merges into this; user requirements R1-R6 are the rubric.

## A. What each production system does (2026)

### A1. salsa crate — persistent caching MERGED (PR #967, Aug 11 2025, `persistence` feature)
Source: github.com/salsa-rs/salsa/pull/967 + hackmd.io/@ibraheemdev/S17Bxowwxg
- ADDITIVE OPT-IN persistence: only queries/structs marked serializable are persisted;
  unmarked intermediate memos are dropped and their input deps are FLATTENED into the
  serialized query's dep list (so correctness survives partial persistence).
- `ValidateInput` trait: after deserialize, inputs re-sync against the real world
  (read file → if changed, set_field → revision bump → downstream invalidation).
  This is exactly tldr's kkt freshness-gate idea, formalized.
- Compile-time consistency: a serializable struct cannot contain a non-serializable
  one (can't persist an ID without its data).
- Limitations (per PR): transitive deps forced serializable, no query hashing yet,
  accumulators not serializable. Followups active — a Jan 1 2026 issue comment marks
  this as THE live thread (driven by ty/Astral's needs; MichaReiser asked for it
  in Apr 2024 explicitly because "persistent cache is essential for CLI + CI").
- FORMAT: serde-based → parse-on-load. NOT zero-copy. This is the gap vs R1.

### A2. Turbopack — filesystem caching SHIPPED on-by-default (Next.js 16.1)
Source: nextjs.org/blog/turbopack-incremental-computation + turbo-persistence README
- Persists the ENTIRE incremental-computation state: dependency graph, aggregation
  graph, and all intermediate value cells. Restart = resume warm. "Over a year of
  dedicated work" to meet quality bar.
- Aggregation graph: coarsened layers over the fine dep graph (hundreds of thousands
  of nodes) so invalidation traversals don't walk the full graph — their answer to
  graph-walk cost at scale.
- turbo-persistence (custom store, LSM-flavored):
  - Immutable files named by sequence number; single CURRENT file holds committed seq
    (same shape as tldr's vector_store generation pattern — independent convergence!).
  - Static Sorted Tables (.sst) + blob files + tombstone files + meta files with
    hash-range + AMQF (approximate membership filter) for fast key routing.
  - Value tiers: INLINE (≤8B in key block) / SMALL (≤4KB shared blocks) /
    MEDIUM (≤64MB dedicated blocks) / BLOB (>64MB separate file).
  - Single write transaction, concurrently fillable from many threads; uncommitted
    leftovers auto-cleaned on startup. Optimized for bulk-write + fast random read.

### A3. rust-analyzer — durabilities (the validation-cost insight)
Source: rust-analyzer.github.io/blog/2023/07/24/durable-incrementality.html (+ issue #4712)
- No persistent cache in-process (still true; #4712 remains open; their hopes ride on
  the new salsa persistence work).
- KEY IDEA TO STEAL — durability tiers: inputs classified volatile/normal/durable
  (user file vs deps vs stdlib). Validation flooding stops at durability boundaries:
  a change to a volatile input doesn't force re-validating the durable universe.
  Without this, even no-op revalidation walks the whole query graph.
- Maps directly to tldr: per-file chunks = normal; project config (Cargo.toml,
  tsconfig, .tldrignore!) = durable inputs whose change invalidates ModuleIndex
  and the compose layer wholesale; compose output = volatile derived layer.

### A4. Cloudflare mmap-sync — zero-copy reads in production (the R1+R3 existence proof)
Source: crates.io/crates/mmap-sync (v2.x)
- rkyv-archived data in shared mmap; wait-free single-writer / many-reader via
  Synchronizer; readers access archived structs IN PLACE — zero deserialization,
  zero copies, RAM cost = page cache only.
- Production-proven (Cloudflare bot-scoring ML feature store). Proves the
  "disk holds archived data, RAM holds only the map + index" model at scale.

### A5. rkyv — the zero-copy format itself
Source: rkyv.org/zero-copy-deserialization.html, docs.rs/rkyv
- Total zero-copy: archived form IS the in-memory form (relative pointers);
  `access` = pointer cast + optional bytecheck validation. No allocation, no parse.
- Cost side: requires Archive derives across the whole type tree (PathBuf/String/
  HashMap-heavy types need rkyv-compatible mirrors); archived types are read-only
  views; schema evolution = explicit versioning discipline.
- bincode/postcard by contrast: compact but ALWAYS parse+allocate on load.

## B. Synthesis: the 2026 architecture for tldr's analysis cache, vs requirements

R1 (near-zero ser/de CPU):
  Chunk payloads = rkyv archives, mmap'd, accessed in place (mmap-sync model).
  Write cost: one archive serialize per changed file (cold-path only).
  Read cost: pointer cast + bytecheck. NO parse on daemon restart or query.
  v1 pragmatic fallback: bincode chunks (serde-native, zero derive churn) are
  acceptable ONLY if the restart budget tolerates parse — the research says the
  2026 answer is rkyv; bincode is the 2020 answer.

R2 (disk is cheap): rkyv archives are larger than bincode (alignment, relative
  pointers) — explicitly acceptable. Value-tier packing (turbo-persistence INLINE/
  SMALL/MEDIUM/BLOB) available later if small-file overhead bites.

R3 (RAM = index only):
  - RAM holds: key index {rel_path → (generation, offset/len, content_hash)} = O(#files),
    + mmap page cache (OS-managed, evictable under pressure — not process heap).
  - CRITICAL EXTENSION the user's framing demands: the COMPOSED graph must also be
    an rkyv artifact on disk (per generation, keyed by corpus digest), mmap'd and
    queried in place — exactly like usearch view(). Otherwise ResidentGraph in an
    ArcSwap re-creates a heap-resident O(V+E) structure and violates R3.
  - Queries walk the archived graph zero-copy; hot pages stay cached, cold pages
    get evicted. Process heap stays O(#files) + small.

R4 (O(n)/O(n log n) audit):
  - Disk: chunks O(source bytes); edges as per-file OUTGOING adjacency lists →
    total O(E) (real-world E ≈ near-linear; the O(n²) trap is adjacency matrices
    or all-pairs precomputation — design stores none); manifest O(#files);
    interning table O(unique strings). Sorted key index → O(n log n) build,
    O(log n) lookup (SST model).
  - RAM: index O(#files); compose working set O(V+E) TRANSIENT during recompose
    (streamed, then archived + dropped); steady-state = page cache only.
  - Aggregation-graph idea (Turbopack) reserved for when invalidation traversal
    itself becomes the bottleneck — not v1.
  - Watch item: reverse-edge index ("who calls X") doubles E on disk — still O(E).

R5 (ignore-first):
  - ONE membership oracle (gitignore + tldrignore + excludes, TLDR-bpf/9w8) applied
    at ENUMERATION; ignored paths are never stat'd into the manifest, never hashed,
    never parsed, never chunked, never watched-through (TLDR-1j2 pre-channel filter),
    never composed. Salsa's ValidateInput formalizes the restart re-sync: validate =
    diff manifest against oracle output; only members get content-hash checks.
  - .tldrignore/.gitignore THEMSELVES are durable inputs (A3): their change
    invalidates membership → manifest diff → chunk add/drop + recompose.

R6 (Codex as research base): pending retry (thread handle alive). Its Q1-Q10
  answers get merged into this annex; disagreements adjudicated against THIS
  evidence base, not vibes.

## C. Sharpened answers to the design doc's open questions

Q3 (format): rkyv for chunk payloads AND the composed-graph artifact is the 2026
  answer (A4/A5). Middle ground struck differently than originally guessed: it's
  not "bincode chunks + rkyv composed graph" — if we pay rkyv derive churn at all,
  pay it once for both. bincode survives only as a v0 spike format.
Q4 (persist composed graph): YES — upgraded from "maybe" to REQUIRED by R3.
  Per-generation, keyed by corpus digest, mmap'd; this is what makes restart O(seconds)
  and steady-state RAM index-only.
Q10 (real salsa crate?): NOT for v1. Evidence: salsa persistence is serde-based
  (violates R1 on the hot path), still limitation-heavy (no query hashing,
  transitive-serializability constraints), and tldr's dependency structure is
  shallow (file → chunk → compose) — it doesn't need salsa's general recursive
  query machinery. STEAL its concepts (additive persistence, ValidateInput,
  dependency flattening, durability tiers) without the crate. Revisit if compose
  ever needs fine-grained incrementality (deep derived-query chains).
Q1 (compose cost): still THE open gate — none of the researched systems make
  full-recompose cheap by magic; Turbopack's answer (aggregation layers) is what
  we'd reach for IF the bench fails the ~2s budget. Bench remains the blocker.

## D. Source inventory (scraped, in .firecrawl/)
- turbopack-incremental.md — Next.js engineering blog (2025/2026 era, FS cache stable)
- turbo-persistence-readme.md — store format spec
- ra-durable.md — durabilities design
- ra-persist-issue.md — rust-analyzer #4712 thread
- salsa-pr967.md — merged persistence prototype PR
- salsa-persist-design.md — ibraheemdev HackMD design doc
- salsa-issue10.md — 7-year persistence RFC thread incl. ty/Astral demand signal
- mmap-sync.md — Cloudflare zero-copy mmap crate

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: PROBLEM STATEMENT v2 + DS DECISION (user direction 2026-06-04): treat as a GRAPH, not a JSON document. Schema adherence to old JSON explicitly waived (JSON survives only as per-response output projection). Priorities: write/index may be slow; READ must be near-free; in-RAM index mandatory; mirror the semantic-store lifecycle exactly ('singular' code flow). DATA STRUCTURES: composed graph = CSR (forward callees + reverse callers, flat offset+edge arrays, rkyv/mmap, O(deg) access); string interning to u32 IDs (kills path/name duplication); FST (fst crate) for in-RAM symbol→node index (prefix/fuzzy for free); file→node-range map in manifest (chunk invalidation meshes with CSR: changed file = contiguous ID range); optional xor/AMQF filter for O(1) negative membership. Query costs: calls=FST+1 CSR row (µs); impact=reverse BFS O(touched); dead=reachability O(V+E); hubs=degree array O(1). Explicitly rejected: graph DBs, heap petgraph, adjacency matrices (O(n2) trap), any deserialize-on-read. Lifecycle mapping: same store root, same generation-commit code, same freshness oracle (TLDR-bpf ignore rules), GraphManager mirrors IndexManager, delta mirrors t8f, status shows 'Graph: warm (N nodes / M edges)'. ~19 of 42 CLI commands benefit (calls/impact/dead/structure/tree/complexity/context/extract/imports/slice/smells + arch/changes/coupling/definition/diff/explain/hubs/temporal + bugbot). Open gates unchanged: Gate 1 compose-cost bench; ID-stability-across-recompose contract = manifest range map.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: REJECTED ALTERNATIVES (ADR note, user-confirmed 2026-06-04): (1) Redis-class network KV: violates R1 (ser/de + socket roundtrip per read, ~50-100µs vs ~100ns mmap read), violates R3 (dataset fully RAM-resident), adds external service to a local CLI tool; our shape is single-writer/local-readers/immutable-generations — mmap IS the IPC. (2) Apache Arrow: columnar/analytical-scan format; our hot path is point lookup + neighbor traversal (row/graph-oriented) — Arrow would force edge-list tables + joins and an extra runtime dep while CSR-in-rkyv already gives equal zero-copy with native Rust types. LEGIT FUTURE NICHE: Parquet/DataFusion sidecar for analytics-flavored commands (temporal, churn/complexity trends) — v3 idea for ~2 commands, not an architecture driver. Principle: match storage layout to access pattern (vectors→HNSW, traversal→CSR, columnar analytics→Arrow).

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: GOVERNING CONSTRAINT (user, 2026-06-04, supersedes anything conflicting): AT MOST TWO data flows in the entire system — Flow 1 = analysis store (per-file rkyv chunks → composed CSR graph + FST index), Flow 2 = existing semantic vector store. Identical lifecycle for both (enumerate via ignore-oracle → build → generation-commit → mmap → per-file delta). ALL CLI commands are READ-ONLY PROJECTIONS over these stores — no command may own storage, caching, or invalidation. Consequences: (1) Q5 ANSWERED: the per-command QueryKey response cache is DELETED — projections over mmap'd CSR are µs-cheap, caching them is pure liability; the per-command invalidation problem ceases to exist structurally. (2) Chunk schema carries ALL per-file facts incl. parse-time metrics (complexity etc.) so complexity/smells project from the same substrate. (3) New command = new projection fn, zero new storage. BOUNDARY: single-function queries (slice/CFG/DFG) are stateless on-demand compute from source — not cached at all, which is consistent with (not an exception to) the two-flow rule.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text:
# IN-HOUSE DESIGN REVIEW — analysis store (TLDR-zde)
# Substitute for the Codex review (provider outage); same brief, same deliverables.
# Reviewer: Claude (session) + advisor pass + Firecrawl web verification. 2026-06-04.

## 1. VERDICT: APPROVE WITH 5 NAMED AMENDMENTS (below). No R1-R6 violations found.

## 2. WEB-VERIFIED FINDINGS (deliverable 4)

(a) rkyv 0.8 for mmap workloads — FIT, with one pattern mandated.
    Source: https://rkyv.org/faq.html
    - The FAQ explicitly blesses the exact pattern we need: full bytecheck validation
      is OPTIONAL; "there are many other ways you can verify your data, for example
      with checksums". Even when validating, "validation is still faster than
      deserializing with other high-performance formats".
    - AMENDMENT A2 derived: validate ONCE after write (bytecheck) + store xxh3
      checksum in the artifact header; subsequent opens verify checksum then use
      access_unchecked. Avoids O(artifact-size) bytecheck on every daemon start —
      without it, a multi-GB artifact would pay a full scan per open and quietly
      violate R1.
    - Risks priced: derive churn across FileIR tree is real but bounded (std types
      incl. PathBuf supported); schema evolution is manual → version field in
      header + rebuild-on-mismatch (already in design).

(b) fst crate — OVERQUALIFIED for our scale, adopt without reservation.
    Sources: https://blog.burntsushi.net/transducers/ , https://github.com/burntsushi/fst
    - Canonical demo indexes 1.6 BILLION keys; the crate is designed around mmap
      ("leverages memory maps to make range queries fast"). Our symbol index is
      30k-1M keys — three to five orders of magnitude below its design point.
    - Bonus: prefix/range/fuzzy (Levenshtein) queries built in — future `tldr`
      symbol-completion features come free.

(c) salsa persistence followups since PR#967 — ACTIVE but architecture unchanged.
    Source: https://github.com/salsa-rs/salsa/pulls?q=is%3Apr+persistence
    - Followup PRs #969, #973, #975, #992, #1027, #1088 (8 closed) — persistence is
      maturing fast, BUT remains serde-based opt-in serialization (parse-on-load).
    - Decision unchanged: steal concepts (ValidateInput, additive persistence,
      durability tiers), skip the crate for v1. Re-evaluate at v2 if compose ever
      needs fine-grained derived-query incrementality.

(d) Prior art: Meta Glean incremental indexing — VALIDATES our simpler v1 choice.
    Source: https://glean.software/blog/incremental/
    - Glean's model: per-unit facts; incremental DB "hides" changed units' facts
      (≈ our chunk overwrite); visibility propagated via OWNERSHIP SETS
      (Elias-Fano coded, interval-mapped, ~7% DB overhead); derived facts carry
      boolean ownership expressions that "can get arbitrarily large".
    - Lesson: ownership/visibility machinery is what you must build if you do NOT
      recompose derived state from scratch. It is substantially complex. Our
      recompose-always v1 (given Gate 1 passes) avoids that entire class.
      Glean's design is the documented ESCALATION PATH if incremental compose is
      ever forced — do not invent a third alternative (Amendment A5).

## 3. GATE ANSWERS (deliverable 2)

GATE 1 — compose-cost bench spec (THE precondition; ~20 lines of instrumentation,
no refactor needed):
  - Instrument build_project_call_graph_v2 with env-gated phase timers
    (TLDR_PHASE_TIMING=1): T_parse = build_indices_parallel (step 4);
    T_compose = steps 5-12 (add_file, build_indices, ModuleIndex,
    ImportResolver/ReExportTracer loop, edge creation).
  - Run on (i) tldr-code (1570 files/~28k fns) and (ii) one 5-10x larger corpus
    (big OSS repo) to get the scaling slope, not just a point.
  - PASS: T_compose ≤ 2s on tldr-code AND slope ≤ O(n log n) between sizes.
  - MARGINAL (2-5s): acceptable — deltas are already debounced/coalesced by the
    serialized worker; recompose at most once per quiet window.
  - FAIL (>5s or superlinear): v1 must add incremental compose; adopt the Glean
    escalation path (per-file edge lists + visibility) — STOP and redesign first.

ID-STABILITY CONTRACT:
  - Node IDs are generation-local. NEVER persisted outside the artifact, NEVER
    returned to clients (responses carry symbol names + file:line). All external
    entry via FST lookup. This single rule eliminates cross-generation dangling-ID
    bugs by construction.
  - Readers pin a generation at open; on unix an unlinked-but-mapped file stays
    valid, so GC can delete eagerly. WINDOWS CAVEAT: mapped files cannot be
    deleted → GC must tolerate failure + retry later (Flow 2 has the same issue;
    share the code).
  - GC policy: keep ≥2 generations + age threshold = the reader-grace contract;
    adopt/extend TLDR-pdb's Flow-2 decision rather than inventing a second one.

MULTI-LANGUAGE (Q6): chunks already carry language; compose runs PER LANGUAGE
  (builder_v2 is per-language today — no cross-language edges exist to lose).
  One manifest with per-language sections: file list + node-range map + CSR
  artifact offset per language. Language-agnostic projections (structure, tree,
  extract) read chunks directly and ignore compose entirely.

Q8 — WHAT SURVIVES FROM salsa.rs:
  - revision counter → generation sequence number under CURRENT (monotonicity
    preserved; same role, better durability).
  - dependents map (input_hash → QueryKeys) → DELETED, replaced structurally by
    the file→node-range manifest. Nothing hash-shaped to maintain.
  - maybe_evict byte caps → generation GC + orphan-chunk GC by manifest
    reachability (mirrors TLDR-za0 on Flow 2).
  - Busy-token/liveness semantics (TLDR-3w5) and warm-ack latency (utj.7):
    untouched by design; recompose runs inside the existing serialized worker.

## 4. ADVERSARIAL PROJECTION HUNT (deliverable 3)

Examined all ~19 commands. NO command requires a third flow. Three findings:
  - `temporal` + `coupling` (co-change): need GIT HISTORY as an additional INPUT.
    Git is an input source read at query time, not a store we own — consistent
    with the two-flow rule the same way source text is. (If churn queries ever
    get slow, the v3 Parquet sidecar note in the ADR covers it.)
  - `explain`/`context`: need source text snippets → read from disk on demand,
    stateless. Fine.
  - `dead`: needs root/entry-point determination → derivable from chunk facts
    (main fns, pub visibility, test attrs). Requires the chunk schema to carry
    visibility/attr flags — ADD to chunk schema checklist.
  Confirmed dependency: `complexity`/`smells` require parse-time metrics in chunks
  (already mandated by the two-flow decision D2).

## 5. TABLE-STAKES OMISSIONS FOUND (deliverable 5)

  - Crash mid-generation-write: covered by CURRENT-flip pattern (Flow 2 proven).
    But chunk overwrites OUTSIDE a generation flip (delta path) need atomic
    rename + manifest update ordering: write chunk → fsync → update manifest →
    fsync → THEN recompose. Spell this out in the implementation issue.
  - Windows mmap deletion semantics (above) — share Flow 2's handling.
  - Concurrent CLI reader during GC — adopt TLDR-pdb contract (above).
  - Artifact integrity on partial disk (ENOSPC mid-write): generation pattern
    handles (incomplete generation never becomes CURRENT); chunk delta path must
    write-to-temp + rename, never truncate-in-place.

## 6. AMENDMENTS (the verdict's conditions)

  A1. Gate-1 phase-timer bench is a HARD precondition to any storage code.
  A2. Integrity pattern: bytecheck-once-after-write + header checksum +
      access_unchecked on subsequent opens.
  A3. Reader-grace/GC: adopt TLDR-pdb semantics for Flow 1; Windows
      deletion-failure tolerance shared with Flow 2.
  A4. Manifest gets per-language sections (Q6); chunk schema checklist grows
      visibility/attr flags (for `dead`) + parse-time metrics (for complexity/smells).
  A5. If Gate 1 fails: escalate to the Glean ownership-set path — documented,
      named, no third invention.

## 7. DISAGREEMENT LOG
  None between this review, the advisor pass, and the research annex. The one
  correction this review adds over earlier session statements: per-open bytecheck
  would have silently violated R1 at scale — caught by verification (a).

comments.text:
# Research annex v2 — second Firecrawl sweep (2026-06-04)
# Focus: library-first opportunities (user philosophy) + production validation of the chunk model.

## F1. GitHub stack-graphs — OUR ARCHITECTURE, VALIDATED AT GITHUB SCALE
Sources: https://github.blog/open-source/introducing-stack-graphs/ ,
         https://arxiv.org/pdf/2211.01224 , https://github.com/github/stack-graphs
- Their index-time rule is verbatim our Gate-0 invariant: "we look at each file
  COMPLETELY IN ISOLATION ... we must ignore [other files] while extracting
  incremental facts". Per-file graphs extracted at push time; merged at query time.
- They go one step FURTHER than our design: resolution is QUERY-TIME PATH-FINDING
  over the merged per-file graphs (stack-based scope resolution), not an eager
  compose. Merge = cheap graph union; the expensive part is lazy and per-query.
- DESIGN IMPACT: this is a SECOND escape hatch for Gate 1, cheaper than Glean's
  ownership sets: if eager compose busts the 2s budget, shift import/call
  resolution to query time over per-file graphs (cacheable per query). Updated
  escalation ladder in F5.
- Their storage: per-file graph blobs (SQLite-backed store in the crate) — the
  chunk model again.

## F2. epserde (Vigna) — candidate to BEAT rkyv for the composed artifact
Sources: https://github.com/vigna/epserde-rs (+ WebGraph-next-gen paper
         https://hal.science/hal-04494627/document , crates.io/crates/webgraph)
- ε-copy deserialization: at load, build a MINUSCULE struct skeleton; all bulk
  (slices/vectors) referenced directly from the mmap backend. Key quote: "the
  performance of the deserialized structure will be IDENTICAL to that of a
  standard, in-memory Rust structure, as references are resolved at
  deserialization time."
- This eliminates rkyv's principal tax: NO Archived* mirror types, no wrapper
  navigation on every access — native &[u32] slices over mmap. For a CSR
  artifact (flat offset/edge/degree arrays + interned string table) this is the
  IDEAL shape. Explicit huge-pages support; MemCase couples instance + backend;
  mmap_rs integration built in.
- Powers webgraph-rs: WebGraph "next generation (is in Rust)" — billions of
  edges, mmap'd, in production research use. CSR-at-scale prior art.
- License: LGPL-2.1 — COMPATIBLE with tldr's AGPL-3.0 (verified Cargo.toml:16).
- DESIGN IMPACT (amendment A6): the format decision is now a TWO-WAY spike, not
  settled: (i) rkyv everywhere, (ii) rkyv for chunks (deep nested FileIR) +
  epserde for the composed CSR artifact (flat arrays, native-speed access).
  Lean (ii): each format used exactly where its strength is; both are
  wiring-only per the library-first philosophy. webgraph's BVGraph compression
  itself is likely overkill (web-graph-specialized gap coding) — epserde + plain
  arrays is the sweet spot.

## F3. ty (Astral) launch data — calibrates the incrementality ceiling
Source: https://astral.sh/blog/ty
- Salsa-based, MIT, shipped. After editing a LOAD-BEARING file in PyTorch:
  diagnostics recompute in 4.7ms (80x faster than Pyright, 500x than Pyrefly).
  10-60x faster than mypy/Pyright WITHOUT caching.
- Validates: fine-grained salsa incrementality delivers millisecond-class
  per-edit updates at huge-repo scale — that is the v2 ceiling if tldr ever
  needs it. ALSO notable: ty runs IN-MEMORY (salsa persistence still maturing) —
  tldr's disk-chunk design is AHEAD of ty on the restart/CI axis, behind it on
  per-edit granularity. Different products, right trade for each.

## F4. oxc-resolver — library-first candidate for part of compose
Sources: https://github.com/oxc-project/oxc-resolver , oxc.rs/docs/guide/usage/resolver
- Rust port of webpack enhanced-resolve, maintained by oxc, used by Rspack/
  Rolldown in production. Handles tsconfig paths/aliases, package.json exports
  maps, monorepo workspace resolution.
- DESIGN IMPACT (candidate only, NOT a v1 decision): tldr's hand-rolled JS/TS
  ModuleIndex/ImportResolver (incl. the VAL-007 workspace-alias logic) could
  potentially be replaced by oxc_resolver — directly per the user's philosophy
  (delete bespoke resolution code, keep wiring). Needs a compatibility spike:
  does oxc_resolver's semantics match builder_v2's resolution contract for the
  languages tldr supports? File as follow-up; do not block v1.

## F5. UPDATED GATE-1 ESCALATION LADDER (supersedes single Glean path)
  1. PASS (compose ≤2s): recompose-always v1 (current design).
  2. MARGINAL: debounce/coalesce recompose (already in design).
  3. FAIL, first resort: stack-graphs model — compose becomes cheap graph union;
     resolution moves to query-time path-finding (cacheable). Production-proven
     at GitHub scale, much simpler than ownership tracking. (F1)
  4. FAIL, heavy artillery: Glean ownership sets / fine-grained salsa (ty-class
     machinery). Only if query-time resolution also proves too slow. (F3 + annex v1)

## F6. AMENDMENT LIST DELTA (extends in-house review's A1-A5)
  A6. Format spike: rkyv-everywhere vs rkyv-chunks + epserde-CSR. Measure load
      cost, access cost (Archived* wrappers vs native slices), derive burden.
  A7. File follow-up issue: oxc_resolver as replacement for hand-rolled JS/TS
      module resolution in compose (library-first; post-v1).

comments.text:
# RESEARCH ROUND 3 — counter-evidence / double-confirmation (2026-06-04)

## C1. MAJOR CORRECTION: stack-graphs is ARCHIVED (Sep 9, 2025)
Verified on repo banner: "This repository was archived by the owner on Sep 9,
2025. It is now read-only" (github.com/github/stack-graphs, final commit = the
archival PR #502). Annex v2 called it "production-proven at GitHub scale" —
that needs qualification: the TECHNIQUE shipped in GitHub precise code-nav and
the arXiv paper documents it, but GitHub has discontinued the OSS project.
Implications:
  (a) The architecture lesson STANDS (per-file isolation at index time, merge
      at query time) — it is documented, shipped prior art.
  (b) Do NOT take a crate dependency on stack-graphs.
  (c) F5 escalation ladder rung 3 reworded: "query-time-resolution PATTERN
      (implement ourselves, per the stack-graphs paper)" — not a library.
  (d) Cautionary signal: GitHub found per-language graph-rule maintenance
      costly enough to wind down. tldr's per-language extraction already
      exists, so the marginal risk differs — but worth remembering.

## C2. epserde constraints CONFIRMED from its own Cons section
  - No references in types (no trees) — fine for flat CSR.
  - ε-copy fields must be TYPE PARAMETERS implementing DeserInner — the one
    artifact type must be written in epserde's generic style (contained burden).
  - Vectors of NON-zero-copy elements deserialize recursively in memory —
    nested FileIR gets ZERO ε-copy benefit → epserde is ONLY a candidate for
    the flat composed artifact; chunks stay rkyv regardless.
  - serialize/deserialize APIs are unsafe-marked; recent API renames
    ("Warning to Previous Users") = mild churn risk. LGPL-2.1 OK with AGPL-3.0.

## C3. rkyv health CONFIRMED: 0.8.8 latest, steady 0.8.x patch cadence.
## C4. fst health CONFIRMED: 20.4M downloads, MIT/Unlicense, BurntSushi.
## C5. oxc_resolver scope CONFIRMED: enhanced-resolve port = JS/TS ONLY.
    It can replace only the JS/TS slice of compose; py/rust/go/java stay ours.

## LOCK RECOMMENDATIONS (post-double-confirmation)
LOCKED NOW:
  - rkyv 0.8.x for per-file chunks (no competitor exists for nested types).
  - fst for the symbol index.
  - CSR layout for the composed graph.
  - Query-time-resolution as escalation PATTERN, not dependency (C1).
SHARPENED PRIOR (decided by the A6 spike, not by fiat):
  - For FLAT u32 arrays, rkyv's archived form is essentially the native form —
    the Archived* tax bites on nested/string types, not PODs. epserde's
    advantage on CSR specifically is therefore likely SMALL, and it costs a
    second framework + generic-style type + unsafe API churn.
  - Default = rkyv-everywhere. epserde adopted only if the spike shows a
    meaningful (>=2x) access/load penalty on the CSR artifact. Either way the
    spike is cheap: one struct, two serializers, one criterion bench.

comments.text:
# IMPLEMENTATION BLUEPRINT (2026-06-04 — the contract for implementation sessions)

DISK: stores/<hash>/analysis/v1/ → chunks/<xxh64-path>.fir (rkyv FileIR incl.
visibility flags + parse-time metrics; header{lang, content_hash, checksum}),
generations/<seq>/{manifest.bin (per-lang sections, file→node-range),
graph.csr (fwd+rev offsets/edges/degrees), strings.tbl, symbols.fst}, CURRENT.

RAM: GraphManager (mirrors IndexManager) = mmap handles + FST + manifest map.
O(#files+symbols). No heap graph, no response cache.

PATHS: cold = oracle→parse∥→chunk+drop-AST→compose→generation→flip.
restart = CURRENT→checksum→mmap→stat-sweep→delta for stale.
edit = watcher(1j2-filtered)→one chunk swap (write-temp+rename, fsync order:
chunk→manifest)→debounced recompose→new gen→reader-grace GC (pdb semantics,
Windows delete-failure tolerant).
query = FST→CSR walk→JSON at the edge; CLI-no-daemon mmaps directly.

CODE: tldr-core/analysis_store/ (chunk io, manifest, compose, CSR, projections);
builder_v2 split parse(file)→FileIR | compose(chunks)→CSR; daemon GraphManager
+ warm step + delta route + 'Graph: warm (N nodes / M edges)' status; DELETE at
cutover: QueryKey cache entries, triple-copy inserts, per-command paths.

PHASES (feature-flagged, Flow 2 untouched):
0 preconditions: TLDR-9ae F1; Gate-1 phase-timer bench; A6 rkyv-vs-epserde
  spike (default rkyv); TLDR-bpf oracle (9w8→1j2/1qv).
1 chunk layer. 2 compose split. 3 artifact+projections (19 cmds).
4 daemon integration. 5 cutover+deletion.

ACCEPTANCE: restart<5s; edit-to-fresh<2s; build peak<8GB; query µs-ms;
idle RAM≈index; CI chunk reuse; 10x project = linear disk, index-only RAM.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: UNIFORMITY RULE + command taxonomy (user-driven, 2026-06-04): Every CLI command must be exactly one of (a) READS a flow (projections, ~35 cmds), (b) JOINS external input against a flow (coverage/diagnostics/fix/verify/churn/temporal — their file:line→symbol mapping becomes ONE shared span→symbol projection over manifest+chunks), (c) OPERATES a flow (daemon/cache/warm/stats/doctor control plane). NO command may do its own file discovery, ignore handling, or symbol search — all enter via oracle→manifest→FST (this generalizes TLDR-9w8: the .tldrignore bug family was caused by N independent walkers). Stateless CFG/DFG cmds (slice/reaching-defs/available/dead-stores/chop) keep stateless COMPUTE but use Flow 1 for NAVIGATION (FST symbol lookup, chunk spans, manifest file lists). CHUNK SCHEMA ADDITIONS (cumulative checklist): visibility/attr flags (dead), parse-time metrics (complexity/smells), function-body fingerprints (clones/dice), comment facts incl. TODOs (todo becomes a pure projection).

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text:
# BENEFITS STATEMENT (what this design buys — measured baseline vs target)

PERFORMANCE
- Daemon restart: 3.5min + 22GB full recompute (measured) -> SECONDS (mmap + checksum, zero parse)
- Single file edit: whole-cache eviction -> next query full rebuild (measured) -> one chunk re-parse + recompose, <=2s end-to-end
- Query latency: cache-miss = minutes -> FST lookup + CSR walk = microseconds, always
- Cold CLI / CI: full build per invocation -> chunks shared across processes AND runs (the ty/Astral CI motivation)

MEMORY
- Build peak: 22.0GB phys_footprint (measured, MALLOC_LARGE) -> <8GB target (streaming parse->chunk->drop-AST; no JSON DOM; no triple copies)
- Steady-state heap: composed graph + JSON blobs -> O(#files + symbols) index only; graph bulk in OS page cache, EVICTABLE under pressure
- Scaling: 10x project = linear disk, index-only RAM growth (R4 audited: no O(n^2) structure exists)

CPU (R1)
- Reads: zero serialization/deserialization, ever (rkyv access_unchecked after one post-write validation)
- Writes: serialize once per CHANGED file only; ignored paths never touch CPU (oracle at enumeration)

CORRECTNESS / MAINTAINABILITY
- The invalidation bug class is STRUCTURALLY GONE: no per-command caches to invalidate; chunk granularity matches change granularity
- Ignore-correctness by construction: one membership oracle, one front door (kills the .tldrignore bug family of TLDR-bpf at the root)
- One lifecycle shared with the semantic store = half the correctness surface; generation commits give crash consistency for free
- New command = one projection function: zero new storage, zero new invalidation, zero new lifecycle
- Library-first: rkyv/fst/ignore/notify maintained upstream; our code is wiring + domain extraction only

PRODUCT
- ~39/63 commands (62%) ride warm stores directly; the rest enter the same door for navigation
- tldr's value prop upgrades from "fast while one daemon stays warm" to "FAST ALWAYS — across restarts, processes, and CI"
- On-disk artifacts are inspectable/debuggable vs today's opaque in-RAM blobs

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: ACCEPTANCE CORRECTION (user challenge 2026-06-04): the '<8GB build peak' was a RETROFIT milestone (F1-only on the old pipeline), NOT the new design's floor. New-design Flow-1 build peak decomposition: parallel-parse transients W×max-per-file (W tunable, ≤1GB at W=8) + compose working set O(V+E) on heap with chunks read via mmap (~100s MB: ~28k fn index entries + interned strings + E×8B edge accumulator) + streaming CSR write (tens of MB). REVISED TARGET: Flow-1 build peak ≤3GB, stretch ≤1GB with bounded rayon width. The old 22GB existed because factors STACKED (all ASTs resident × duplicated indices × JSON DOM 5-15x × serialized bytes); the design removes the stacking. REMAINING STUBBORN PEAK: Flow 2 cold embed measured ~11.5GB resident (ONNX arenas+batching) — sequential phases mean daemon peak = max(phase peaks), so post-redesign the daemon's worst moment is the EMBED phase, untouched by Flow 1 work. Own knobs: embed batch size, ONNX arena config, intra-op threads — must be a separate TLDR-yll/k8s work item, not hidden behind Flow-1 numbers.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: STATUS CORRECTION (adversarial self-review + advisor, 2026-06-04): DOWNGRADE all 'LOCKED' architecture items to PROPOSED-PENDING-GATE-1. Rationale: Gate 1 (compose cost) decides whether recompose-based design is viable at all; locking around an unrun measurement manufactured false certainty. ALSO: (1) compose becomes LAZY/DEMAND-DRIVEN in the core model — edits swap chunks only; compose on first query-after-dirty (kills per-edit write amplification; Gate-1 budget becomes per-query-after-edit); (2) 'all 35 commands are projections' is PARTLY UNVERIFIED — taint (inter-procedural dataflow) and clones/dice need explicit verification; (3) builder_v2 split requires a DIFFERENTIAL GATE (old vs new pipeline identical edges on fixtures); (4) rkyv schema bumps force full chunk rebuilds — chunk-fact scope creep has a real cost. SCOPE FORK to put before the user: Path A surgical (F1 + rayon width bound + index dedup + naive generation persistence — days, captures most measured pain) vs Path B full two-flow store (weeks+, full benefits). Path A can precede and de-risk B. NEXT ACTIONS (before any further design work): run F1 (TLDR-9ae) and the Gate-1 phase-timer bench.

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: GATE-1 RESULT: FAIL — AND THE CORE ASSUMPTION WAS INVERTED (measured 2026-06-04, branch build, 3 runs, isolated worktree, daemon SIGSTOPped to avoid contention). rust, 745 files, 20,320 funcs, 26,312 edges: scan ~14ms / parse ~2.8s / COMPOSE ~223s (221-225s across runs) / total ~226s. Compose = 98.8% of the build — 110x over the 2s pass line. The design premise 'parse expensive+memoizable, compose cheap' is BACKWARDS: memoizing parse saves ~1% of build time. Recompose-always is DEAD as designed. CRITICAL FOLLOW-ON: 223s / 26,312 edges = ~8.5ms per edge = pathological (ty checks all of home-assistant in 5.3s; rust-analyzer resolves workspaces in seconds) — likely an ALGORITHMIC DEFECT in resolution, not inherent cost. Profiler evidence (sample, 10s mid-compose): compose is SINGLE-THREADED (rayon pool idle in psynch_cvwait), one tight hot function cluster (offsets 0xd7ee4-0xd7fbc, ~250B span) + heavy malloc/realloc/memmove/memcmp = string-building/comparison in a tight loop. Symbolication blocked by strip=true; unstripped rebuild in progress to NAME the hot function. NEW SEQUENCING: optimize/parallelize compose hot path FIRST — if compose drops to ~2-5s, recompose-always becomes viable again and the architecture stands; if not, escalate (query-time resolution / incremental compose). F1 (TLDR-9ae) landed in the same commit (100f465).

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: GATE-1 ROOT CAUSE NAMED (profiled with symbols, 2026-06-05): tldr_core::callgraph::resolution::resolve_global_fuzzy_match → FuncIndex::find_by_name (types.rs:509) is a LINEAR SCAN over the whole (module,name)-keyed HashMap with a string compare per entry — called up to 2x per call site from the fallback resolution path (+ resolve_global_free_function). ~call-sites × ~40k entries (20k funcs × module aliases) = billions of comparisons = the 223s compose. Top-of-stack: core::str::pattern::StrSearcher::new + str::find dominating on-CPU; compose confirmed SINGLE-THREADED (rayon pool parked in psynch_cvwait). FIX (~20 lines, classic missing secondary index): add by_name: HashMap<String,Vec<(module,name)>> maintained in insert()/merge(); find_by_name becomes O(matches). EXPECTED: compose 223s → single-digit seconds, which would RESURRECT recompose-always and re-validate the chunk-store economics — pending re-bench. ClassIndex doc comment ('O(1) lookup') shows original intent; find_by_name never got its index. Fix awaiting user authorization (current authorization covered F1 + bench only).

comments.author: Udhayakumar
comments.text: PROVENANCE (verified via git, 2026-06-05): find_by_name linear scan dates to d64dead 2026-04-21 ('Initial release') and is byte-identical on origin/main today. comments.text: GATE-1 FIX ROUND 2 (committed 8ca8f0c, 2026-06-05): RustReceiverIndex — per-file binding/impl index replacing per-call-site whole-file text scans in apply_type_resolution. Measured: compose 221-225s → 144-148s (-31%), counts byte-identical (745/20320/26312), exhaustive differential test + 1039 callgraph tests green. comments.text:
# GATE-1 CAMPAIGN RESULT (overnight 2026-06-05, commits 100f465 / 8ca8f0c / 106738d)
